question: A patient has sudden weakness predominantly in the contralateral lower limb with urinary incontinence and subtle behavioral changes. Which vascular territory best explains these findings?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: The anterior cerebral artery supplies the paracentral lobule including M1 and S1 for the lower extremity and prefrontal cortex; lesions cause contralateral lower-extremity weakness, cortical sensory loss, incontinence, and frontal behavioral signs.

Wrong: MCA lesions primarily affect face and upper extremity motor/sensory cortex; if the deficit were trunk/leg predominant, MCA would be less likely. It would be correct if deficits involved face and arm more than leg.

Wrong: PCA lesions classically produce visual field defects and thalamic syndromes; they do not primarily produce isolated contralateral lower-extremity weakness and frontal behavioral changes. It would be correct if visual field loss or thalamic signs predominated.

Wrong: Watershed infarcts between ACA and MCA cause proximal arm/trunk weakness (man-in-a-barrel), not isolated lower-extremity weakness with frontal signs. It would be correct if proximal upper limb/trunk weakness were the main deficit.

Wrong: Lacunar lenticulostriate strokes cause pure motor or sensorimotor deficits without prominent frontal behavioral changes or incontinence. It would be correct if there were a pure motor hemiparesis without cortical signs.

question: Which clinical syndrome is most consistent with a small deep infarct affecting the posterior limb of the internal capsule producing contralateral pure motor weakness without cortical signs?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Lacunar infarcts in the PLIC (often anterior choroidal or deep MCA branches) disrupt CST/CBT producing contralateral UMN weakness without cortical signs, matching the pure motor lacunar syndrome.

Wrong: Pure sensory lacunes arise from thalamic (VPL/VPM) infarcts causing hemisensory loss rather than isolated motor weakness. It would be correct if sensory loss without motor deficit were present.

Wrong: Ataxic hemiparesis includes ipsilateral ataxia plus weakness due to corticopontine involvement; pure motor hemiparesis lacks the ataxic component. It would be correct if prominent ipsilateral limb ataxia accompanied weakness.

Wrong: Sensorimotor lacunes involve both motor and sensory fibers (PLIC dorsal) producing combined deficits; pure motor hemiparesis lacks significant sensory loss. It would be correct if both modalities were impaired.

Wrong: Thalamic pain (Dejerine-Roussy) follows thalamic infarcts causing chronic neuropathic pain and sensory disturbances, not isolated motor weakness. It would be correct if painful hemisensory symptoms developed after stroke.

question: A patient presents with fluent, nonsensical spontaneous speech, markedly impaired comprehension, and a right superior quadrantanopia. Which cortical region is most likely affected?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Left inferior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Correct: The left (dominant) MCA inferior division supplies Wernicke’s area and adjacent temporal-parietal cortex; lesions produce fluent aphasia with poor comprehension and visual field defects from optic radiation involvement.

options.text: Right inferior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Wrong: Right inferior MCA lesions cause neglect and visual deficits but not dominant-language Wernicke’s aphasia. It would be correct if the patient had hemispatial neglect rather than language impairment.

options.text: Left superior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Wrong: Left superior MCA lesions more often produce Broca’s (nonfluent) aphasia and contralateral face/arm weakness rather than fluent comprehension deficits. It would be correct if nonfluent speech and motor deficits predominated.

Wrong: PCA lesions cause visual field defects and can cause alexia without agraphia when splenium involved, but fluent Wernicke-type aphasia localizes to MCA inferior division. It would be correct if isolated visual cortex or splenial signs were present.

Wrong: ACA lesions produce contralateral leg weakness and frontal signs, not fluent receptive aphasia with superior quadrantanopia. It would be correct if lower-extremity motor and frontal behavioral signs were dominant.

question: Which syndrome best matches a hypertensive patient with sudden onset contralateral hemisensory loss followed weeks later by chronic burning pain on the affected side?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Infarction of inferolateral thalamic arteries (VPL/VPM) causes contralateral hemisensory loss acutely and can evolve into chronic central post-stroke pain (Dejerine-Roussy).

Wrong: Pure sensory lacunes cause hemisensory loss but do not necessarily progress to chronic thalamic pain; progression to neuropathic pain indicates thalamic involvement specifically. It would be correct if no chronic pain developed.

Wrong: Pure motor lacunes affect CST pathways causing weakness, not hemisensory loss or chronic neuropathic pain. It would be correct if the presentation were isolated motor deficit without sensory symptoms.
Wrong: Ataxic hemiparesis includes ipsilateral ataxia and weakness from corticopontine involvement, not thalamic pain. It would be correct if limb ataxia accompanied weakness.

Wrong: Cortical sensory strokes involve parietal cortex with higher-order sensory loss (astereognosis), not classic thalamic neuropathic pain; thalamic vascular lesion is required for Dejerine-Roussy. It would be correct if cortical discrimination deficits predominated without neuropathic pain.

question: A patient has contralateral facial and arm weakness with lower facial paralysis sparing the forehead and expressive language impairment. Which arterial division is most likely involved?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Left superior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Correct: The superior division of the dominant (left) MCA supplies Broca’s area and lateral M1 for face/arm; lesions cause contralateral face/arm weakness with lower facial paralysis and expressive (Broca) aphasia.

options.text: Right superior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Wrong: Right superior MCA lesions produce contralateral face/arm weakness but not dominant-language Broca’s aphasia. It would be correct if the patient had nonverbal motor deficits without language impairment.

options.text: Left inferior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Wrong: Left inferior MCA lesions produce Wernicke’s receptive aphasia and sensory deficits rather than expressive Broca-type aphasia with prominent motor weakness. It would be correct if comprehension were primarily impaired.

Wrong: ACA lesions affect lower extremity motor/sensory cortex and frontal signs, not face/arm motor cortex or Broca’s area. It would be correct if leg weakness and frontal behavioral changes were present.

Wrong: Deep perforator (lacunar) strokes can cause pure motor deficits but typically spare cortical language functions; Broca’s aphasia implies cortical involvement in MCA superior division. It would be correct if language were intact and only motor pathways were affected.

question: Which vascular lesion most likely produces contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: PCA infarcts involving visual cortex produce contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing due to dual blood supply to the occipital pole.

Wrong: MCA lesions can cause visual field defects via optic radiations but classically produce contralateral face/arm deficits; macular sparing is characteristic of PCA cortical visual loss. It would be correct if optic radiation involvement produced the field cut without macular sparing.

Wrong: ACA infarcts affect medial frontal and paracentral lobule, not primary visual cortex; they do not typically cause homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing. It would be correct if occipital lobe were involved.

Wrong: Lateral striate (lenticulostriate) strokes affect internal capsule/striatum causing motor deficits, not cortical visual field defects. It would be correct if deep visual pathway structures were affected, which is uncommon.

Wrong: Watershed infarcts produce proximal limb/trunk weakness and higher-order deficits, not classic macular-sparing homonymous hemianopia from occipital cortex ischemia. It would be correct if occipital watershed regions were specifically ischemic.

question: An elderly hypertensive patient develops sudden pure contralateral facial, arm, and leg weakness without cortical signs. Imaging shows a small deep hemorrhagic lesion in the basal ganglia region. Which arterial group is most implicated?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Hypertensive hemorrhage or occlusion of lenticulostriate arteries supplying the basal ganglia/internal capsule produces lacunar pure motor hemiparesis.

Wrong: Posterior choroidal branches supply thalamic and posterior structures causing visual and memory deficits; they are less associated with pure motor basal ganglia hemorrhages. It would be correct if thalamic signs predominated.

Wrong: ACA branches supply medial frontal and paracentral lobule causing leg-predominant deficits, not deep basal ganglia hemorrhages causing global hemiparesis. It would be correct if deficits were leg-predominant with frontal signs.

Wrong: Cortical MCA branches cause cortical deficits including aphasia or cortical sensory loss rather than isolated deep motor hemiparesis from basal ganglia hemorrhage. It would be correct if cortical signs were present.

Wrong: Thalamoperforate arteries supply medial thalamus and can cause sensory and arousal deficits; they do not typically cause isolated pure motor hemiparesis from basal ganglia hemorrhage. It would be correct if thalamic sensory or arousal changes were present.

question: A patient with a dominant-hemisphere stroke cannot read words aloud but can write normally and name objects. Which specific disconnection best explains this deficit?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Lesion of the splenium of the corpus callosum (PCA territory) disconnects visual input from the language-dominant hemisphere, producing alexia without agraphia (cannot read aloud but can write and name).

options.text: Left inferior frontal language cortex lesion
options.rationale: Wrong: Left inferior frontal (Broca) lesions impair expressive speech and writing, not isolated reading with preserved writing; it would be correct if expressive language production were impaired.

options.text: Left superior temporal auditory cortex lesion
options.rationale: Wrong: Superior temporal (Wernicke) lesions impair comprehension and naming, not isolated alexia without agraphia; it would be correct if auditory comprehension and naming were disrupted.
Wrong: Right parietal lesions cause neglect and visuospatial deficits, not dominant-hemisphere alexia without agraphia. It would be correct if hemispatial neglect were the main deficit.

Wrong: Anterior callosal lesions affect motor interhemispheric transfer and may cause apraxia, not isolated reading impairment; it would be correct if bilateral motor coordination deficits were present.

question: Which syndrome is most likely when a patient has transient episodes of focal neurologic deficits that fully resolve within 24 hours and herald risk for future infarction?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: TIAs are transient focal ischemic episodes resolving within 24 hours and are medical emergencies because they predict risk of subsequent stroke.

Wrong: Lacunar infarcts produce persistent focal deficits due to small deep infarcts, not transient deficits resolving within 24 hours. It would be correct if deficits persisted and imaging showed small deep infarct.

Wrong: Migraine aura can cause transient focal neurologic symptoms but is distinguished by migrainous features and not necessarily increased immediate stroke risk; it would be correct if headache and migrainous history were present.
Wrong: Postictal (Todd) paresis can be transient but is associated with preceding seizure activity; it would be correct if witnessed convulsive or focal seizure preceded the deficit.

Wrong: Subdural hematomas cause progressive focal deficits and altered consciousness over days to weeks, not brief transient deficits resolving within 24 hours. It would be correct if imaging showed a subdural collection causing mass effect.

question: A patient demonstrates left hemispatial neglect and right gaze preference after an acute event. Which vascular territory is most consistent with these findings?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Right inferior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Correct: Right (non-dominant) MCA inferior division lesions involving parietal cortex produce left hemineglect and often rightward gaze preference related to parietal and frontal eye field dysfunction.

options.text: Left inferior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Wrong: Left inferior MCA lesions produce Wernicke’s aphasia and left visual deficits, not left hemispatial neglect; it would be correct if receptive language deficits were present instead.
Wrong: Right ACA lesions cause contralateral leg weakness and frontal signs, not classic hemispatial neglect; it would be correct if leg-predominant motor and frontal behavioral changes were seen.
Wrong: Left PCA lesions cause visual field defects and alexia without agraphia, not hemispatial neglect; it would be correct if visual cortex or splenium signs predominated.

Wrong: Watershed infarcts cause proximal limb weakness and transcortical deficits, not focal hemispatial neglect from parietal cortex; it would be correct if proximal arm/trunk weakness were the main feature.

question: Which lacunar syndrome typically presents with dysarthria and clumsy hand weakness in a hypertensive patient with small deep pontine or internal capsule lesion?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Dysarthria-clumsy hand lacunar syndrome arises from small lesions in ventral pons or corona radiata affecting corticobulbar and corticospinal fibers, producing dysarthria and hand clumsiness.
Wrong: Pure motor lacunes cause global contralateral weakness without the prominent dysarthria and isolated hand clumsiness; it would be correct if speech were intact and only motor weakness present.
Wrong: Pure sensory lacunes produce hemisensory loss from thalamic infarcts, not dysarthria or hand clumsiness; it would be correct if sensory deficits predominated.
Wrong: Ataxic hemiparesis includes ipsilateral limb ataxia plus weakness, not specifically dysarthria with clumsy hand; it would be correct if limb ataxia accompanied weakness.

Wrong: Sensorimotor lacunes involve both motor and sensory deficits, whereas dysarthria-clumsy hand emphasizes bulbar and hand motor involvement; it would be correct if both modalities were equally affected.

question: A patient has contralateral homonymous hemianopia and hemisensory loss with mild contralateral weakness after a single vascular event involving thalamoperforate branches. Which major artery is most likely involved?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: PCA stem lesions that include thalamoperforate branches can affect thalamus and occipital lobe producing contralateral hemisensory loss, homonymous hemianopia, and sometimes hemiparesis as described in the lecture.

Wrong: MCA stem lesions produce contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, and homonymous hemianopia but typically spare thalamic structures supplied by thalamoperforate PCA branches; it would be correct if lateral thalamocortical pathways were involved instead.

Wrong: ACA proximal occlusion causes contralateral leg weakness and frontal signs rather than combined visual and thalamic sensory deficits; it would be correct if leg-predominant motor and frontal behavioral changes were present.

Wrong: Lenticulostriate occlusions cause pure motor or sensorimotor lacunes in basal ganglia/internal capsule, not occipital visual field defects; it would be correct if only motor pathways were affected without visual loss.

Wrong: Watershed ischemia produces proximal limb weakness and higher-order deficits, not the combination of thalamic sensory loss and homonymous hemianopia from PCA stem involvement. It would be correct if global hypoperfusion with proximal weakness were the context.

question: A patient with sudden right homonymous hemianopia and alexia without agraphia likely has a lesion affecting which combination of structures?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Left occipital cortex and splenium of corpus callosum
options.rationale: Correct: Left PCA infarct involving occipital cortex causes right homonymous hemianopia; involvement of the splenium disconnects visual input from language areas producing alexia without agraphia.

options.text: Left inferior frontal gyrus and optic radiations
options.rationale: Wrong: Left inferior frontal (Broca) lesions impair expressive language but do not produce alexia without agraphia; optic radiation lesions cause visual field cuts but splenial disconnection is required for alexia without agraphia. It would be correct if expressive language and visual pathways were both disrupted without callosal involvement.

options.text: Right parietal lobe and right optic tract
options.rationale: Wrong: Right parietal and optic tract lesions produce left-sided visual and spatial deficits, not right homonymous hemianopia with alexia in the dominant hemisphere. It would be correct if the patient were right-hemisphere dominant for language, which is uncommon.

options.text: Left thalamus and left temporal pole
options.rationale: Wrong: Thalamic and temporal pole lesions cause sensory and memory/language disturbances but do not classically produce alexia without agraphia with homonymous hemianopia; it would be correct if splenial or occipital involvement were present.

Wrong: Bilateral occipital lesions cause cortical blindness or bilateral visual field loss but would not produce alexia without agraphia unless the splenium were specifically involved. It would be correct if callosal disconnection coexisted.

question: Which clinical sign indicates involvement of the frontal eye fields producing an ipsilateral gaze preference toward the lesion side?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Lesions of frontal eye fields (often in MCA territory) produce conjugate gaze deviation toward the side of the lesion (ipsilateral), as noted in the lecture.
Wrong: Homonymous hemianopia reflects occipital or optic radiation lesions, not frontal eye field dysfunction; it would be correct if visual pathway structures were involved.

Wrong: Lower facial weakness sparing the forehead indicates an upper motor neuron lesion affecting corticobulbar fibers, not specifically frontal eye fields; it would be correct if facial motor pathways were implicated.
Wrong: An upgoing plantar response indicates UMN involvement but is not specific for frontal eye field lesions; it would be correct if corticospinal tract involvement were the focus.
Wrong: Pupillary findings relate to brainstem or autonomic pathways, not frontal eye fields; it would be correct if midbrain or autonomic dysfunction were present.

question: A patient with hypertension develops sudden contralateral hemisensory loss without weakness. Imaging localizes a small infarct in the ventral posterolateral nucleus. Which lacunar syndrome label fits best?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Infarction of VPL/VPM thalamic nuclei (inferolateral thalamic arteries) produces contralateral hemisensory loss characteristic of pure sensory lacunar syndrome.
Wrong: Pure motor lacunes affect CST pathways causing weakness, not isolated hemisensory loss from thalamic nuclei. It would be correct if motor pathways were disrupted instead.

Wrong: Dysarthria-clumsy hand involves corticobulbar and corticospinal fibers producing speech and hand motor deficits, not isolated thalamic sensory loss. It would be correct if bulbar and hand motor signs were present.
Wrong: Sensorimotor lacunes involve both motor and sensory fibers (PLIC dorsal); pure sensory lacune is limited to sensory nuclei like VPL. It would be correct if both modalities were impaired.
Wrong: Ataxic hemiparesis includes ipsilateral ataxia and weakness from corticopontine involvement, not isolated thalamic sensory loss. It would be correct if ataxia plus weakness were present.

question: A patient presents with sudden dense left hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, and left homonymous hemianopia after an embolic event. Which vascular occlusion best accounts for this constellation?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Right middle cerebral artery stem occlusion
options.rationale: Correct: MCA stem occlusion affects internal capsule (PLIC), thalamocortical fibers, and optic radiations producing contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, and homonymous hemianopia as described in the lecture.

options.text: Right posterior cerebral artery stem occlusion
options.rationale: Wrong: PCA stem occlusion causes visual field defects and thalamic signs but typically less prominent contralateral hemiplegia than MCA stem occlusion; it would be correct if visual loss and thalamic deficits predominated without dense motor loss.

options.text: Left middle cerebral artery superior division occlusion
options.rationale: Wrong: Left MCA superior division causes face/arm weakness and Broca’s aphasia but not the full dense hemiplegia and homonymous hemianopia seen with an MCA stem lesion; it would be correct if deficits were limited to face/arm and expressive language.

options.text: Right anterior cerebral artery proximal occlusion
options.rationale: Wrong: Right ACA occlusion causes contralateral leg weakness and frontal signs, not the global hemiplegia and visual field loss described; it would be correct if leg-predominant weakness and frontal behavioral changes were present.

Wrong: Watershed infarcts produce proximal limb weakness and higher-order deficits from global hypoperfusion, not the focal dense hemiplegia with homonymous hemianopia typical of an MCA stem embolus. It would be correct if there were a history of systemic hypotension and bilateral proximal weakness.

question: Which clinical feature distinguishes cortical sensory loss from thalamic hemisensory loss on examination?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Cortical sensory loss (parietal cortex) causes higher-order deficits like impaired stereognosis and two-point discrimination, whereas thalamic lesions produce primary hemisensory loss without cortical discrimination deficits.

options.text: Burning neuropathic pain on affected side
options.rationale: Wrong: Burning central pain is characteristic of thalamic (Dejerine-Roussy) syndrome, not cortical sensory loss. It would be correct if chronic neuropathic pain developed after thalamic infarct.

Wrong: Hemiparesis with UMN signs indicates corticospinal tract involvement, not specifically cortical sensory discrimination deficits. It would be correct if motor pathways were involved alongside sensory loss.

options.text: Visual field defect in contralateral hemifield
options.rationale: Wrong: Visual field defects localize to occipital or optic radiation lesions, not distinguishing cortical versus thalamic sensory loss. It would be correct if visual pathway structures were affected.

Wrong: Preserved discrimination with numbness suggests peripheral neuropathy or thalamic primary sensory loss; cortical sensory loss typically impairs discrimination. It would be correct if primary sensory pathways were affected without cortical processing deficits.

question: A patient with a dominant-hemisphere MCA stem infarct is most likely to present with which language and attentional profile?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Global aphasia with contralateral neglect absent
options.rationale: Correct: A dominant (left) MCA stem lesion can produce global aphasia due to extensive cortical involvement; contralateral neglect is more typical of non-dominant hemisphere lesions, so neglect would be absent.

options.text: Wernicke aphasia with severe left neglect
options.rationale: Wrong: Wernicke’s aphasia arises from inferior division lesions of the dominant hemisphere and is not associated with left neglect; left neglect suggests right hemisphere non-dominant lesions. It would be correct if the lesion were in the dominant inferior MCA and the patient had atypical hemispheric dominance.

options.text: Broca aphasia with profound right neglect
options.rationale: Wrong: Broca’s aphasia results from superior division dominant MCA lesions and is not typically accompanied by right neglect; right neglect implies left hemisphere non-dominant dysfunction, which is uncommon. It would be correct if the lesion were in the non-dominant hemisphere producing neglect and coincident expressive deficits due to atypical dominance.

options.text: Alexia without agraphia and bilateral neglect
options.rationale: Wrong: Alexia without agraphia localizes to left PCA splenial involvement, not MCA stem; bilateral neglect is rare and would require extensive bilateral lesions. It would be correct if PCA splenial and bilateral parietal involvement occurred.

options.text: Transcortical motor aphasia with left-sided neglect
options.rationale: Wrong: Transcortical motor aphasia is associated with ACA watershed or anterior frontal lesions, not MCA stem; left-sided neglect suggests right hemisphere involvement. It would be correct if watershed or frontal lesions were present with atypical dominance.

question: Which vascular territory is most vulnerable during systemic hypotension and produces proximal arm and trunk weakness when injured?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Watershed zones between ACA and MCA are vulnerable to global hypoperfusion and when injured produce weakness of proximal upper limbs and trunk (man-in-a-barrel syndrome).

Wrong: MCA superior division affects face and arm more than trunk and is less specifically vulnerable to systemic hypotension; it would be correct if focal face/arm deficits predominated without watershed pattern.

Wrong: PCA occipital lesions cause visual field defects and are not the classic site for proximal arm/trunk weakness from hypoperfusion. It would be correct if visual deficits were the main presentation.

Wrong: Deep perforator lacunes cause focal motor or sensory deficits but are not the watershed regions most susceptible to systemic hypotension. It would be correct if small deep infarcts from hypertension were the cause.

Wrong: AChA lesions produce PLIC motor and visual deficits but are not the classic watershed zone causing proximal arm/trunk weakness from global hypoperfusion. It would be correct if internal capsule involvement produced the deficits described.

question: A patient has contralateral lower facial weakness sparing the forehead and later develops hyperreflexia and an upgoing plantar response on that side. What does this temporal evolution indicate about lesion localization?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Initial cortical lesion with later UMN signs
options.rationale: Correct: Early lower facial weakness sparing forehead suggests an upper motor neuron cortical lesion; later emergence of hyperreflexia and Babinski indicates evolving UMN corticospinal tract signs consistent with cortical or subcortical CST involvement.

options.text: Peripheral facial nerve palsy evolving to UMN signs
options.rationale: Wrong: Peripheral (LMN) facial palsy affects forehead and lower face and does not evolve into UMN hyperreflexia; it would be correct if forehead were involved and no UMN signs developed.

options.text: Brainstem lesion causing initial facial weakness
options.rationale: Wrong: Brainstem LMN facial nucleus lesions produce ipsilateral whole-face weakness including forehead and are often accompanied by other cranial nerve signs, not delayed contralateral UMN signs. It would be correct if ipsilateral cranial nerve deficits and gaze palsies were present.

options.text: Neuromuscular junction disorder causing reflex changes
options.rationale: Wrong: NMJ disorders cause fluctuating weakness without UMN hyperreflexia or Babinski signs; it would be correct if reflexes were reduced and fatigability prominent.

options.text: Spinal cord lesion producing facial weakness
options.rationale: Wrong: Spinal cord lesions do not produce facial weakness; facial motor pathways are supraspinal. It would be correct only if a concurrent supranuclear lesion existed.

question: Which artery most commonly supplies the lateral striate perforators implicated in hypertensive lacunar hemorrhages of the basal ganglia and internal capsule?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: The lenticulostriate arteries are deep perforators arising from the MCA and are prone to hypertensive hemorrhage causing lacunar lesions in basal ganglia/internal capsule.

Wrong: ACA perforators supply medial frontal structures and paracentral lobule, not the lenticulostriate basal ganglia territory; it would be correct if medial frontal or leg-predominant deficits were present.
Wrong: PCA perforators supply thalamic and occipital regions, not the lenticulostriate basal ganglia; it would be correct if thalamic or visual deficits predominated.

Wrong: AChA supplies PLIC and can cause motor deficits but the classic lenticulostriate basal ganglia hemorrhages are from MCA branches; it would be correct if imaging localized the lesion specifically to AChA territory.

Wrong: Thalamoperforate arteries supply medial thalamus and midline structures, not the lateral lenticulostriate basal ganglia; it would be correct if thalamic sensory/arousal deficits were the main features.

question: A patient with a right non-dominant MCA inferior division infarct is most likely to exhibit which prominent clinical deficit?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Right (non-dominant) MCA inferior division lesions involving parietal cortex commonly produce left-sided neglect and related attentional deficits.
Wrong: Broca’s aphasia localizes to the dominant (usually left) MCA superior division; it would be correct if the dominant hemisphere superior division were affected.

Wrong: Alexia without agraphia localizes to left PCA splenial lesions disconnecting visual input from language areas, not right MCA inferior division. It would be correct if left splenial involvement occurred.
Wrong: Leg-predominant weakness localizes to ACA territory, not MCA inferior division. It would be correct if ACA territory were involved.
Wrong: Macular-sparing hemianopia is typical of PCA occipital cortex lesions, not MCA inferior division; it would be correct if occipital pole were ischemic.

question: Which clinical scenario best exemplifies an ataxic hemiparesis lacunar syndrome?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Contralateral weakness with ipsilateral limb ataxia
options.rationale: Correct: Ataxic hemiparesis results from lacunar lesions affecting CST and corticopontine fibers producing contralateral weakness plus ipsilateral limb ataxia, as described in the lecture.

Wrong: Isolated facial numbness suggests a focal sensory lacune or cortical sensory lesion, not ataxic hemiparesis which includes motor and ataxic components. It would be correct if only sensory pathways were involved.

options.text: Bilateral leg weakness with urinary incontinence
options.rationale: Wrong: Bilateral leg weakness and incontinence suggest ACA or sagittal sinus pathology, not unilateral ataxic hemiparesis. It would be correct if medial frontal/paracentral lobule were affected bilaterally.
Wrong: Memory impairment and confabulation point to medial diencephalic or temporal lobe dysfunction, not ataxic hemiparesis. It would be correct if limbic structures were involved.
Wrong: Pure motor lacunes lack the ataxic component; ataxic hemiparesis specifically includes ipsilateral ataxia with weakness. It would be correct if ataxia were absent.

question: Which thalamic vascular territory, when infarcted, is most likely to produce decreased arousal, impaired memory, and confabulation?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Paramedian (anteromedial) thalamic infarcts involving medial diencephalic structures produce decreased arousal, memory impairment, and confabulation as listed in the lecture table.

Wrong: Inferolateral (thalamogeniculate) infarcts cause hemisensory loss and Dejerine-Roussy pain, not primary arousal and memory deficits. It would be correct if hemisensory pain syndromes were the main features.

Wrong: Posterior choroidal infarcts affect LGN/MGN and can cause visual field defects and memory issues but the classic arousal/confabulation picture is paramedian. It would be correct if visual and memory deficits predominated with less arousal impairment.

Wrong: Tuberothalamic infarcts cause fluctuating arousal and executive dysfunction but confabulation and severe memory impairment are most characteristic of paramedian lesions; it would be correct if fluctuating orientation and executive failure were emphasized.

Wrong: Thalamoperforate branches supply medial thalamus and can impair arousal but the paramedian territory is most classically linked to confabulation and severe memory loss. It would be correct if medial thalamic arousal deficits were the dominant feature.

question: A patient with sudden onset right facial and arm cortical-type sensory loss and right inferior quadrantanopia likely has ischemia in which cortical loop?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Left MCA inferior division parietal loop
options.rationale: Correct: Left MCA inferior division supplies parietal cortex including intraparietal sulcus and optic radiations (parietal loop) producing contralateral cortical sensory loss and inferior quadrantanopia.

options.text: Left MCA superior division frontal loop
options.rationale: Wrong: Superior division affects frontal lobe and motor cortex causing face/arm weakness and Broca’s area involvement, not parietal cortical sensory loss with inferior quadrantanopia. It would be correct if motor and expressive language deficits predominated.
Wrong: Right PCA occipital lesions cause left visual field defects but not left cortical sensory loss; it would be correct if visual deficits without cortical sensory impairment were present.

Wrong: ACA paracentral lesions produce contralateral leg weakness and sensory loss, not face/arm cortical sensory loss and inferior quadrantanopia. It would be correct if lower-extremity deficits were the main findings.

Wrong: Deep perforator lacunes cause pure motor or sensorimotor deficits without cortical discrimination loss or quadrant visual field defects; it would be correct if only deep motor/sensory pathways were affected.

question: Which clinical feature would most strongly suggest a cortical (parietal) sensory deficit rather than a thalamic sensory deficit?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Impaired stereognosis with preserved primary sensation
options.rationale: Correct: Cortical parietal lesions impair higher-order sensory processing (stereognosis) while primary modalities (touch, pain) may be relatively preserved, distinguishing cortical from thalamic lesions.

options.text: Severe burning neuropathic pain on affected side
options.rationale: Wrong: Severe burning central pain is characteristic of thalamic (Dejerine-Roussy) lesions, not cortical parietal deficits. It would be correct if thalamic infarct produced chronic neuropathic pain.
Wrong: Hemiparesis without sensory loss suggests pure motor pathway involvement (PLIC) rather than cortical sensory dysfunction. It would be correct if motor pathways were selectively affected.
Wrong: Macular-sparing visual field defects localize to occipital cortex (PCA), not parietal cortical sensory processing. It would be correct if occipital lobe were involved.

Wrong: Global aphasia indicates extensive dominant hemisphere cortical involvement including language areas, not specifically parietal sensory discrimination deficits. It would be correct if language and multiple cortical functions were impaired.

question: A patient with sudden onset dysarthria and clumsy right hand after a small pontine lesion is most likely to have involvement of which fiber systems?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Corticobulbar and corticospinal fibers in ventral pons
options.rationale: Correct: Dysarthria-clumsy hand syndrome arises from lesions in ventral pons affecting corticobulbar (speech) and corticospinal/corticopontine fibers (hand clumsiness), matching the lecture description.
Wrong: Medial lemniscus and spinothalamic involvement produce sensory deficits, not dysarthria and isolated hand clumsiness; it would be correct if prominent sensory loss were present.

options.text: Inferior cerebellar peduncle and vestibular nuclei
options.rationale: Wrong: Inferior cerebellar peduncle lesions cause ipsilateral ataxia and vestibular signs, not dysarthria with clumsy hand from corticobulbar/corticospinal disruption. It would be correct if cerebellar signs predominated.

Wrong: Facial and abducens nucleus lesions produce LMN cranial nerve palsies with whole-face weakness and gaze palsy, not the dysarthria-clumsy hand lacunar pattern. It would be correct if ipsilateral cranial nerve deficits were present.

Wrong: Thalamic nuclei lesions cause sensory and higher-order deficits, not the pontine corticobulbar/corticospinal pattern causing dysarthria and hand clumsiness. It would be correct if hemisensory loss and thalamic pain developed.

question: Which finding on exam most strongly localizes a lesion to the dominant hemisphere rather than the non-dominant hemisphere?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Aphasia affecting language production or comprehension
options.rationale: Correct: Aphasia (Broca’s or Wernicke’s) localizes to the dominant hemisphere, as the lecture emphasizes that aphasias localize to the dominant side.
Wrong: Left neglect typically indicates right (non-dominant) hemisphere lesions; it would be correct if the lesion were in the non-dominant parietal lobe.
Wrong: Leg-predominant weakness suggests ACA territory involvement and does not by itself indicate hemispheric dominance. It would be correct if ACA territory in either hemisphere were affected.
Wrong: Macular-sparing hemianopia localizes to occipital cortex (PCA) and does not specify dominance; it would be correct if occipital lobe were involved regardless of hemisphere.
Wrong: Frontal release signs indicate frontal lobe dysfunction but are not specific to dominant hemisphere; it would be correct if bilateral frontal pathology produced these signs.

question: A patient with a left MCA deep territory infarct presents with contralateral hemiparesis and aphasia. Which structures are most likely lesioned?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Internal capsule posterior limb and adjacent corona radiata
options.rationale: Correct: MCA deep territory (lateral striate arteries) supplies the PLIC and corona radiata; lesions produce contralateral UMN weakness and, in the dominant hemisphere, aphasia when larger cortical connections are involved.

options.text: Superior temporal gyrus and Wernicke area
options.rationale: Wrong: Superior temporal gyrus (Wernicke) is supplied by MCA inferior division and causes receptive aphasia without primary motor hemiparesis; it would be correct if fluent aphasia with comprehension deficits predominated without dense motor loss.

options.text: Paracentral lobule and medial frontal cortex
options.rationale: Wrong: Paracentral lobule (ACA territory) affects lower extremity motor/sensory function and frontal signs, not the deep MCA territory producing hemiparesis and aphasia. It would be correct if leg-predominant weakness and frontal behavioral changes were present.
Wrong: Occipital pole (PCA) causes visual field defects and is not the primary site for hemiparesis with aphasia; it would be correct if visual field loss were the main deficit.

Wrong: Basilar paramedian branches supply brainstem structures causing cranial nerve and long-tract signs, not cortical aphasia with contralateral hemiparesis; it would be correct if brainstem signs were present.

question: Which clinical pattern is most characteristic of an anterior choroidal artery infarct?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, homonymous hemianopia
options.rationale: Correct: AChA infarcts classically affect the PLIC and optic tract/radiations producing contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, and homonymous hemianopia as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Isolated expressive aphasia without motor deficit
options.rationale: Wrong: Isolated expressive aphasia localizes to Broca’s area in MCA superior division, not AChA; it would be correct if cortical Broca’s area were selectively ischemic.

options.text: Pure sensory loss with thalamic pain syndrome
options.rationale: Wrong: Pure sensory loss and thalamic pain arise from inferolateral thalamic artery infarcts, not AChA; it would be correct if VPL/VPM thalamic nuclei were involved.

options.text: Bilateral leg weakness and frontal disinhibition
options.rationale: Wrong: Bilateral leg weakness and frontal signs suggest ACA territory or sagittal sinus pathology, not unilateral AChA infarct; it would be correct if medial frontal/paracentral lobule were affected bilaterally.

Wrong: Macular-sparing hemianopia is typical of PCA occipital lesions; AChA infarct produces visual field loss plus motor and sensory deficits. It would be correct if occipital cortex alone were ischemic.

question: A patient with sudden onset right-sided weakness and sensory loss has elevated reflexes and an upgoing plantar response two days later. Which pathophysiologic process best explains the delayed emergence of hyperreflexia?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Initial neuronal shock followed by UMN disinhibition
options.rationale: Correct: Early after cortical or subcortical injury there can be transient hypotonia (neuronal shock); over days, loss of descending inhibitory corticospinal input leads to UMN signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski), consistent with lecture descriptions of evolving UMN disease.
Wrong: Peripheral demyelination causes decreased reflexes and sensory changes, not delayed hyperreflexia; it would be correct if peripheral neuropathy signs (reduced reflexes) were present.

options.text: Delayed intracerebral hemorrhagic conversion causing LMN signs
options.rationale: Wrong: Hemorrhagic conversion can worsen deficits but would not produce LMN signs; LMN signs include decreased reflexes and flaccidity. It would be correct if flaccid weakness and decreased reflexes developed.

options.text: Metabolic encephalopathy resolving to reveal baseline reflexes
options.rationale: Wrong: Metabolic encephalopathy typically causes global depressed reflexes and altered consciousness, not focal delayed UMN hyperreflexia; it would be correct if global metabolic factors were the primary cause.

options.text: Spinal shock from acute cord compression
options.rationale: Wrong: Spinal shock causes initial areflexia followed by hyperreflexia but is localized to spinal cord injury, not cortical stroke; it would be correct if there were acute spinal cord compression.

question: Which vascular territory lesion most commonly produces contralateral lower-extremity weakness and frontal behavioral changes including abulia?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: ACA supplies the paracentral lobule (M1/S1 lower extremity) and medial prefrontal cortex; lesions cause contralateral leg weakness and frontal signs such as abulia and perseveration.

Wrong: MCA superior division affects face/arm motor cortex and Broca’s area, not leg-predominant weakness and frontal abulia; it would be correct if face/arm deficits and expressive aphasia were present.

Wrong: PCA lesions cause visual field defects and thalamic signs, not primary lower-extremity weakness with frontal behavioral changes; it would be correct if visual or thalamic deficits predominated.

Wrong: Lenticulostriate lacunes cause pure motor or sensorimotor deficits without prominent frontal behavioral changes; it would be correct if isolated motor deficits without frontal signs were present.

Wrong: Watershed infarcts produce proximal arm/trunk weakness and transcortical deficits, not isolated leg weakness with frontal abulia; it would be correct if proximal upper limb weakness were the main feature.

question: A patient with sudden onset left visual field cut and right-sided hemisensory loss is found to have a lesion involving the lateral geniculate nucleus and adjacent thalamic nuclei. Which arterial branch is most likely responsible?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected thalamic vascular regions and deficits (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Posterior choroidal arteries supply the LGN and adjacent thalamic nuclei; lesions can produce contralateral visual field defects and hemisensory impairment as described in the lecture table.
Wrong: Inferolateral arteries supply VPL/VPM causing hemisensory loss and thalamic pain but not primarily the LGN visual pathway; it would be correct if visual pathway involvement were absent.

Wrong: Paramedian branches affect medial thalamic nuclei causing arousal and memory deficits, not LGN-mediated visual field cuts; it would be correct if decreased arousal and memory impairment were present.

Wrong: Lenticulostriate arteries supply basal ganglia/internal capsule and cause motor deficits, not LGN or thalamic visual nucleus lesions; it would be correct if pure motor hemiparesis were the presentation.

Wrong: AChA supplies PLIC and optic tract portions but LGN and adjacent thalamic nuclei are classically supplied by posterior choroidal branches; it would be correct if optic tract rather than LGN were primarily involved.

question: Which syndrome is most likely when a patient has contralateral weakness of proximal upper limbs and trunk with relative sparing of distal hand function after a period of systemic hypotension?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Watershed (borderzone) infarct between ACA and MCA
options.rationale: Correct: ACA-MCA watershed infarcts preferentially affect cortical areas representing trunk and proximal upper limb, producing the man-in-a-barrel pattern after global hypoperfusion as described in the lecture.

Wrong: MCA superior division affects face and arm regions including distal hand, not the proximal trunk-predominant weakness seen in watershed infarcts. It would be correct if distal arm and face were primarily weak.

Wrong: Lacunar infarcts cause focal deep motor or sensorimotor deficits, not the proximal limb/trunk weakness pattern from watershed ischemia. It would be correct if a small deep motor deficit without cortical features were present.
Wrong: PCA occipital infarcts cause visual field defects, not proximal limb weakness; it would be correct if visual deficits were the main issue.

Wrong: AChA infarcts produce PLIC motor and visual deficits but do not classically cause the proximal arm/trunk-predominant weakness of watershed infarcts. It would be correct if internal capsule motor fibers and optic tract were involved.

question: Which exam maneuver best detects extinction and hemispatial neglect indicating non-dominant parietal lobe dysfunction?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Extinction on double simultaneous stimulation is a classic test for hemispatial neglect and non-dominant parietal lobe dysfunction, as noted in the lecture under neglect assessment.
Wrong: Serial sevens tests calculation and dominant angular gyrus function, not extinction or hemispatial neglect; it would be correct if assessing calculation and dominant parietal function.
Wrong: Two-point discrimination assesses primary and cortical sensory discrimination, not specifically extinction or neglect; it would be correct if testing cortical sensory loss.
Wrong: Digit span assesses attention and working memory, often frontal or diffuse dysfunction, not hemispatial neglect; it would be correct if global attention deficits were suspected.
Wrong: Snellen testing assesses ocular visual acuity, not cortical neglect or extinction; it would be correct if ocular pathology were suspected.

question: A patient with a left MCA inferior division infarct demonstrates severe impairment in comprehension but preserved repetition. Which aphasia subtype does this pattern most closely resemble?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Transcortical sensory aphasia features poor comprehension with preserved repetition and can result from lesions that spare the perisylvian repetition pathways but affect surrounding temporal-parietal cortex, consistent with some inferior MCA lesions.

Wrong: Wernicke’s aphasia has poor comprehension and impaired repetition; preserved repetition argues against classic Wernicke’s and favors transcortical sensory aphasia. It would be correct if repetition were also impaired.
Wrong: Broca’s aphasia is nonfluent with relatively preserved comprehension and impaired repetition; it would be correct if speech production were nonfluent and effortful.

Wrong: Global aphasia shows severe impairment of comprehension and repetition and nonfluent speech; preserved repetition excludes global aphasia. It would be correct if all language modalities were severely impaired.

Wrong: Anomic aphasia primarily causes word-finding difficulty with relatively preserved comprehension and repetition, not severe comprehension impairment; it would be correct if only naming were affected.

question: Which vascular lesion is most likely when a patient presents with contralateral hemiparesis affecting face and arm more than leg, and mild cortical sensory loss of the same distribution?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: MCA cortical territory supplies lateral M1 and S1 representing face and upper limb; lesions produce contralateral face/arm-predominant weakness and cortical sensory loss as described in the lecture.
Wrong: ACA lesions affect lower extremity motor/sensory cortex producing leg-predominant deficits, not face/arm-predominant weakness. It would be correct if leg weakness were dominant.
Wrong: PCA lesions cause visual field defects and thalamic signs, not primary face/arm motor and cortical sensory deficits. It would be correct if visual cortex or thalamus were primarily affected.
Wrong: Deep perforator lacunes cause pure motor or sensorimotor deficits without cortical sensory discrimination loss; it would be correct if only deep motor pathways were involved.
Wrong: Watershed infarcts produce proximal arm/trunk weakness, not lateral face/arm-predominant deficits; it would be correct if proximal limb weakness were the main feature.

question: Which clinical constellation best fits a PCA infarct that includes thalamoperforate branches?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Contralateral hemisensory loss and homonymous hemianopia
options.rationale: Correct: PCA stem lesions involving thalamoperforate branches can produce contralateral hemisensory loss (thalamic involvement) and homonymous hemianopia (occipital involvement), as noted in the lecture.

options.text: Contralateral leg weakness and frontal disinhibition
options.rationale: Wrong: Leg weakness and frontal signs localize to ACA territory, not PCA; it would be correct if ACA were involved.
Wrong: Broca’s aphasia and facial weakness localize to dominant MCA superior division, not PCA; it would be correct if MCA superior division were affected.

options.text: Pure motor hemiparesis without sensory loss
options.rationale: Wrong: Pure motor hemiparesis arises from PLIC lacunes (AChA or deep MCA), not PCA stem lesions; it would be correct if internal capsule were selectively involved.

Wrong: Alexia without agraphia can result from left PCA splenial involvement but the addition of hemisensory loss indicates broader PCA stem/thalamic involvement. It would be correct if only splenial occipital disconnection occurred without thalamic signs.

question: A patient with a small lacunar infarct in the corona radiata presents with contralateral weakness and sensory loss. Which label best describes this lacunar presentation?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: Lacunae in the corona radiata or superior PLIC affecting both motor and sensory fibers produce a sensorimotor lacunar syndrome with contralateral hemiparesis and hemisensory loss.
Wrong: Pure motor lacunes affect CST pathways without significant sensory loss; sensorimotor lacunes include both modalities. It would be correct if sensory function were intact.
Wrong: Pure sensory lacunes arise from thalamic infarcts causing hemisensory loss without motor weakness; it would be correct if motor strength were preserved.

Wrong: Dysarthria-clumsy hand involves bulbar and hand motor dysfunction, not combined widespread sensorimotor deficits in corona radiata. It would be correct if dysarthria and isolated hand clumsiness were the main features.

Wrong: Ataxic hemiparesis includes ipsilateral ataxia plus weakness, not the combined contralateral sensorimotor loss typical of corona radiata lacunae. It would be correct if ataxia accompanied the weakness.

question: Which clinical sign would most strongly suggest involvement of the primary motor cortex hand area rather than a deep internal capsule lesion?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Faciobrachial weakness with cortical sensory loss
options.rationale: Correct: Cortical M1 hand area lesions often produce facio-brachial weakness with accompanying cortical sensory deficits (discrimination loss), whereas internal capsule lesions produce pure motor deficits without cortical sensory signs.

Wrong: Hyperreflexia is a UMN sign seen with both cortical and internal capsule lesions and does not distinguish cortical hand area from deep lesions. It would be correct if additional localization clues were absent.
Wrong: Pure sensory loss suggests thalamic or cortical sensory area involvement, not primary motor cortex hand area; it would be correct if motor strength were preserved.
Wrong: Visual field defects localize to occipital cortex or optic radiations, not motor cortex; it would be correct if visual pathways were involved.
Wrong: Bilateral leg weakness suggests ACA territory or sagittal sinus issues, not focal hand area cortical lesion; it would be correct if medial frontal/paracentral lobule were affected.

question: Which artery is most likely implicated in a lacunar pure motor stroke that spares cortical signs and arises from hypertension-related small vessel disease?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.rationale: Correct: The anterior choroidal artery supplies the ventral PLIC motor fibers; lacunar infarcts here (or deep MCA perforators) commonly produce pure motor hemiparesis in hypertensive small vessel disease.

Wrong: Posterior choroidal branches supply LGN and posterior thalamic structures causing visual and memory deficits, not classic pure motor lacunes. It would be correct if visual or thalamic signs predominated.
Wrong: Inferolateral arteries supply VPL/VPM causing pure sensory lacunes and thalamic pain, not pure motor strokes. It would be correct if hemisensory loss were the main feature.

Wrong: ACA cortical branches supply medial frontal/paracentral lobule causing leg-predominant deficits and frontal signs, not deep pure motor lacunes from small vessel disease. It would be correct if leg weakness and frontal signs were present.

Wrong: While lenticulostriate perforators do cause lacunar motor strokes, the classic pure motor lacune often involves PLIC supplied by AChA or deep MCA; if the question specified lenticulostriate origin, that option would be correct.

question: Which combination of deficits would most strongly suggest a large MCA stem infarct rather than a more distal branch occlusion?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, homonymous hemianopia
options.rationale: Correct: MCA stem occlusion affects PLIC, thalamocortical fibers, and optic radiations producing dense contralateral hemiplegia, hemisensory loss, and homonymous hemianopia, a constellation indicating proximal MCA stem involvement.

options.text: Isolated expressive aphasia without motor deficit
options.rationale: Wrong: Isolated expressive aphasia suggests a distal superior division MCA cortical lesion (Broca’s area) rather than a proximal stem occlusion; it would be correct if motor pathways were spared.

options.text: Pure sensory loss with thalamic pain only
options.rationale: Wrong: Pure sensory loss with thalamic pain localizes to thalamic (PCA inferolateral) lesions, not MCA stem; it would be correct if thalamic nuclei were the sole site of infarction.

options.text: Proximal arm weakness with preserved face strength
options.rationale: Wrong: Proximal arm weakness suggests watershed ACA-MCA borderzone involvement, not MCA stem which typically affects face and arm together; it would be correct if watershed hypoperfusion were the cause.
Wrong: Macular-sparing visual loss indicates PCA occipital cortex involvement, not MCA stem; it would be correct if occipital pole ischemia were isolated.

question: Which clinical feature is most characteristic of a lesion in the paracentral lobule supplied by the ACA?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Contralateral lower-extremity weakness and sensory loss
options.rationale: Correct: The paracentral lobule (medial M1/S1) supplied by ACA controls lower-extremity motor and sensory function; lesions produce contralateral leg weakness and cortical sensory deficits.
Wrong: Face and arm motor cortex are lateral and supplied by MCA, not ACA; it would be correct if MCA territory were involved.
Wrong: Fluent receptive aphasia localizes to dominant MCA inferior division (Wernicke), not ACA paracentral lobule; it would be correct if temporal-parietal cortex were affected.
Wrong: Macular-sparing hemianopia indicates PCA occipital cortex involvement, not ACA; it would be correct if occipital pole were ischemic.
Wrong: Hemispatial neglect is a non-dominant parietal sign typically from MCA inferior division lesions, not ACA paracentral lobule; it would be correct if non-dominant parietal cortex were involved.

question: Which vascular territory lesion is most likely to produce motor aprosodia (loss of emotional prosody) rather than language aphasia?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected cerebral artery territorial syndromes (recall vascular supply and functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Right superior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Correct: Motor aprosodia localizes to the non-dominant (usually right) frontal opercular regions supplied by the MCA superior division; dominant hemisphere lesions produce aphasia instead.

options.text: Left superior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Wrong: Left superior MCA lesions produce Broca’s aphasia (language production deficits) rather than aprosodia; it would be correct if expressive language impairment were present.
Wrong: Right PCA lesions cause visual and thalamic deficits, not motor aprosodia; it would be correct if visual field or thalamic signs predominated.

options.text: Left inferior division of middle cerebral artery
options.rationale: Wrong: Left inferior MCA causes Wernicke’s receptive aphasia, not aprosodia; it would be correct if fluent comprehension deficits were present.

Wrong: ACA lesions cause leg weakness and frontal behavioral signs but not classically motor aprosodia; it would be correct if medial frontal structures affecting prosody were involved, which is uncommon.

question: A patient with a small lacunar infarct develops chronic neuropathic burning pain on the contralateral body months after the event. Which mechanism best explains this delayed pain syndrome?
cited_learning_objective: Recognize presentations of selected lacunar syndromes (recall vascular supply and basic etiology, e.g. functional structures lesioned)

options.text: Central post-stroke thalamic pain due to deafferentation
options.rationale: Correct: Thalamic infarcts (inferolateral arteries) can cause deafferentation of sensory pathways leading to central post-stroke neuropathic pain (Dejerine-Roussy), often emerging weeks to months later.

Wrong: Diabetic small-fiber neuropathy causes distal symmetric burning pain but would not be temporally linked to a focal lacunar stroke; it would be correct if neuropathy preceded or explained the symptoms independently.

options.text: Complex regional pain syndrome after limb immobilization
options.rationale: Wrong: CRPS follows peripheral injury and immobilization with autonomic signs, not focal thalamic deafferentation after lacunar stroke; it would be correct if there were a preceding limb trauma and autonomic changes.

options.text: Postherpetic neuralgia from varicella zoster reactivation
options.rationale: Wrong: Postherpetic neuralgia follows dermatomal shingles and is peripheral in origin, not central thalamic pain; it would be correct if a dermatomal rash and zoster history were present.

options.text: Spinal cord syrinx causing central pain
options.rationale: Wrong: A syrinx causes segmental central pain and motor/sensory signs at spinal levels, not the hemisensory burning pain pattern following a thalamic lacune; it would be correct if spinal imaging showed a syrinx corresponding to symptoms.
